title: Building Your First RAG System: A Practical Guide
author: AI Systems Engineering Team
excerpt: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is the most practical way to add domain knowledge to LLMs. Here's how to build one from scratch.

content.text: RAG stands for Retrieval-Augmented Generation. It's a pattern where you: (1) retrieve relevant documents from a knowledge base, (2) add them to the prompt, and (3) let the LLM generate an answer based on that context.
Why not just fine-tune? Because knowledge changes. Your product docs get updated, policies change, new research gets published. RAG lets the LLM access fresh information without retraining.
A basic RAG system has four components:

items: Document processor — loads, chunks, and cleans your documents
Embedding model — converts chunks into dense vectors
Vector database — stores vectors and performs similarity search
Generation layer — the LLM that answers using retrieved context

content.text: Chunking is critical. Too small: loses context. Too large: dilutes relevance. For most documents, 256-512 tokens with 10-20% overlap works well. For code, chunk by function. For conversations, chunk by turn.

def chunk_text(text, chunk_size=512, overlap=50):
    chunks = []
    start = 0
    while start < len(text):
        end = start + chunk_size
        chunks.append(text[start:end])
        start = end - overlap
    return chunks

Use a sentence embedding model like sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2 (fast, cheap) or OpenAI's text-embedding-3-large (best quality). The embedding converts each chunk into a 384-3072 dimensional vector.

For prototypes, use ChromaDB or FAISS. For production, consider Pinecone, Weaviate, or pgvector. The key operation is approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search — finding the most similar vectors efficiently.
The magic happens in how you format the retrieved context. A good prompt template:

Once basic RAG works, you can improve it with: re-ranking (using a cross-encoder to score retrieved chunks), query expansion (generating multiple search queries from the user's question), and hybrid search (combining vector similarity with keyword matching).
